title: Quiz: Creation and Annihilation Operators
description: Test your understanding of creation and annihilation operators

questions.question: What is the result of applying an annihilation operator to the vacuum state?
questions.explanation: Annihilating the vacuum gives the zero vector (not the vacuum state!). You cannot remove a particle that doesn't exist. a|0⟩ = 0.

questions.question: What is the commutation relation for bosonic operators?
questions.explanation: Bosons satisfy the canonical commutation relation [aᵢ, a†ⱼ] = aᵢa†ⱼ - a†ⱼaᵢ = δᵢⱼ. The anticommutator {,} is for fermions.

questions.question: Why is (c†ᵢ)² = 0 for fermions?

options.text: Because of the Pauli exclusion principle encoded in anticommutation

questions.explanation: From the anticommutation relation {c†ᵢ, c†ᵢ} = 2(c†ᵢ)² = 0, we get (c†ᵢ)² = 0. This means you cannot create two fermions in the same state—the Pauli exclusion principle!

questions.question: What is c†₁c†₂|0⟩ for fermions when c†₁ and c†₂ are swapped?
questions.explanation: For fermions, c†ᵢc†ⱼ = -c†ⱼc†ᵢ (anticommutation). Swapping the order of creation operators introduces a minus sign. This is the origin of wavefunction antisymmetry.

questions.question: The vacuum state |0⟩ is the same as the zero vector 0.
questions.explanation: False! The vacuum |0⟩ is a normalized state with ⟨0|0⟩ = 1. The zero vector 0 has norm zero. a|0⟩ = 0 (zero vector), but |0⟩ ≠ 0.

questions.question: For bosons, what is a†a|n⟩?
questions.explanation: a†a is the number operator. Acting on |n⟩: a†a|n⟩ = a†(√n|n-1⟩) = √n · √n|n⟩ = n|n⟩. It returns the occupation number.

questions.question: What is the physical interpretation of a†|n⟩?

options.text: Remove a particle from state n
Add a particle to the state, increasing occupation by 1

questions.explanation: The creation operator a† adds one particle to the state. a†|n⟩ = √(n+1)|n+1⟩, increasing the occupation number from n to n+1.

questions.question: Which statements are true for fermionic operators? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Occupation numbers can only be 0 or 1
The order of operators affects the sign

questions.explanation: For fermions: (a) nᵢ ∈ {0,1} due to Pauli exclusion; (b) {cᵢ,cⱼ} = cᵢcⱼ + cⱼcᵢ = 0; (d) swapping operators changes sign. Option (c) is wrong—for i≠j, cᵢc†ⱼ = -c†ⱼcᵢ.
